title: MQTT Client Disconnect Reason Analysis
app: HiveMQ Splunk Extension, HiveMQ broker logs
spl:
index=ot sourcetype="hivemq:log"
| search disconnect OR DISCONNECT OR "Client ID"
| rex field=_raw "(?i)client[_ ]?id[:=]\s*(?<client_id>[^\s,;]+)"
| eval category=case(
    match(_raw,"(?i)timeout|idle|keep.?alive"), "timeout",
    match(_raw,"(?i)admin|kick|forced"), "admin_kick",
    match(_raw,"(?i)reset|eof|closed"), "network_error",
    match(_raw,"(?i)not.?authorized|bad.?user"), "auth_failure",
    true(), "other"
  )
| stats count by category, client_id, host
| sort - count
description: Categorized disconnect reasons separate clean shutdowns from timeouts, kicks, and protocol errors — shortening MTTR for unstable OT clients.
value: Categorized disconnect reasons separate clean shutdowns from timeouts, kicks, and protocol errors — shortening MTTR for unstable OT clients.
implementation: Align rex patterns with HiveMQ log format for your version. If reason codes are numeric, maintain a lookup mapping code to category. Filter out expected maintenance windows.
visualization: Bar chart (disconnects by category), Pie chart (category mix), Table (top client_ids), Timeline (disconnect bursts).
knownFalsePositives: Client disconnect reason spikes during keep-alive tuning, client upgrades, or roaming wireless clients.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: HiveMQ Splunk Extension, HiveMQ broker logs.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=ot` `sourcetype="hivemq:log"` client disconnect lines.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Align rex patterns with HiveMQ log format for your version. If reason codes are numeric, maintain a lookup mapping code to category. Filter out expected maintenance windows.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=ot sourcetype="hivemq:log"
| search disconnect OR DISCONNECT OR "Client ID"
| rex field=_raw "(?i)client[_ ]?id[:=]\s*(?<client_id>[^\s,;]+)"
| eval category=case(
    match(_raw,"(?i)timeout|idle|keep.?alive"), "timeout",
    match(_raw,"(?i)admin|kick|forced"), "admin_kick",
    match(_raw,"(?i)reset|eof|closed"), "network_error",
    match(_raw,"(?i)not.?authorized|bad.?user"), "auth_failure",
    true(), "other"
  )
| stats count by category, client_id, host
| sort - count
```

Understanding this SPL

**MQTT Client Disconnect Reason Analysis** — Categorized disconnect reasons separate clean shutdowns from timeouts, kicks, and protocol errors — shortening MTTR for unstable OT clients.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=ot` `sourcetype="hivemq:log"` client disconnect lines. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): HiveMQ Splunk Extension, HiveMQ broker logs. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: ot; **sourcetype**: hivemq:log. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=ot, sourcetype="hivemq:log". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Applies an explicit `search` filter to narrow the current result set.
• Extracts fields with `rex` (regular expression).
• `eval` defines or adjusts **category** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by category, client_id, host** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Step 3 — Validate
Run the search over a period with known traffic, then compare with the MQTT broker UI or `mosquitto_sub` for the same topics and clients. Spot-check counts against broker access and metrics logs.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar chart (disconnects by category), Pie chart (category mix), Table (top client_ids), Timeline (disconnect bursts).
grandmaExplanation: We read client disconnect reasons so we can tell a clean shutdown from a network or keep-alive problem.
